content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies that do awkward human well?

**Score:** 43
**Body:** I'm not wanting only awkward teenagers, but adults as well. The one that I think did very well is Napoleon Dynamite. I had rewatched it recently, after years of not seeing it. Pretty much everybody is awkward, for one reason or another. It's very well done (imo).

It doesn't need to be a comedy, or a coming of age story either. (It can be, but doesn't have to be) Feel free to recommend me any genre, decade, language, etc. As long as it fits what I'm looking for.

TV recs are fine as well. I know this is a Subreddit for movies, but shows are fine too.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Beau is Afraid 
2. Friendship (2024)

Ingrid Goes West (2017)

Muriel’s Wedding (1994)
  - I love Muriel's Wedding! 
3. Hot Rod
4. Pretty much any Wes Anderson movie
5. Punch-Drunk Love!!!! Also Swiss Army Man, Kumiko the Treasure Hunter, Friendship (2024).


Also TV but Nathan For You and The Rehearsal. Nathan himself plays up his own awkwardness, but there are also a lot of Genuine Irl Weird People™ that pop up.
6. Lars and the Real Girl 

Please Stand By 
7. Scott Pilgram vs the World

May
8. Waiting For Guffman
9. Ghost World, perhaps

## 2. Movies with very tight scripts that waste no time?

**Score:** 34
**Body:** Looking for movies with very tight scripts that waste no time. Movies with no added fat. Almost like you can’t walk away for 2min without missing something major in the story. 

Some movies that come to mind are 12 Angry Men, No Country for Old Men, Momento, Back to the Future, Casablanca, The Prestige. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tremors has no fat.  It's a perfect B movie.
2. Spotlight
3. Sanctuary (2022)  
Run Lola run (1998)  
Palm springs (2020)  
Barbarian (2022)  
The Dinner Game (Le Dîner de cons - 1998)
  - Was going to suggest Run Lola Run.  It’s so good and so efficient. 
4. Hot Fuzz. It’s always Hot Fuzz. 
  - Yes, yes it is. Hot Fuzz is imo a perfect movie. 
5. Glengarry Glen Ross
6. Thelma and Louise
7. Mad Max Fury road

8. * Fargo (1996)
* Chinatown (1974)
* Michael Clayton (2007)

## 3. Any really funny comedy movie suggestions?

**Score:** 27
**Body:** I’m looking for a genuinely hilarious movie something that actually makes you laugh out loud, not just mildly amusing 

that is so perfect to watch in the night

i feel like comedies from the 90s and early 2000s had a different kind of charm which it make it special for me (I don’t mind recommendations for movies after the 2000s era tho)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A Fish Called Wanda (1988). I was caught off guard by how hard it made me laugh.

Kevin Kline won an Oscar for it too which is pretty crazy for a purely comic role. Absolutely deserved it as well.
  - It’s k-k-k-Ken, and he’s c-c-c-coming to k-k-k-kill me!!!
2. Blazing Saddles
3. Shaun of the Dead 
4. Tucker and Dale vs. Evil. Don't look up anything about it before you watch.
5. Eurotrip
6. Game Night (2018)
7. Monty Python and the Meaning of Life

A Fish Called Wanda

There’s Something About Mary

Tropic Thunder

Kiss Kiss Bang Bang

War of the Roses

Borat (first movie)

Dogma

Bowfinger

L.A. Story

The Incredibles
8. Bridesmaids. Tommy Boy.
9. Airplane!

## 4. What Are the Most Unpredictable Mindfuck Movies That Actually Challenge You?

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I’m not looking for basic twist-ending movies or mainstream “mind-bending” stuff.
I want films that are genuinely unpredictable—where the narrative structure, reality, or character identity breaks down in a way that forces you to actively think. The kind of movies that feel disorienting, abstract, or even confusing on first watch, but become more rewarding the more you analyze them.
Examples of what I’m talking about: Mulholland Drive, Primer, Synecdoche, New York, Upstream Color, Inland Empire.
What I’m not looking for:
Simple twist movies where everything is explained at the end
Straightforward psychological thrillers
Anything too conventional or formulaic
I’m open to:
Arthouse / experimental
Nonlinear storytelling
Identity/reality distortion
Films that require multiple viewings
The more challenging and unpredictable, the better.
If you’ve got something that genuinely made you question what you were watching, drop it.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Donnie Darko, Memento, eXistenZ, Coherence, and Eraserhead
2. For a fun one, under the silver lake is quite good.

But nothing can top synecdoche New York, that’s my number one. Have you seen I’m thinking of ending things? Not as good but similar enough to be worth your time.

For horror, cure and perfect blue are also excellent movies you will probably enjoy.
3. Frailty. There’s a Catholic/religious aspect to it that makes the turn of events even more intriguing in my opinion
4. Sorry to Bother You

Mother!
5. Triangle
6. Jacob’s ladder
7. You should check out the films of Brandon Cronenberg, David Cronenberg's son. His films like Possessor or Infinity Pool might fuck with you a little bit 
8. The director's cut of The Butterfly Effect (2004)
9. Man bites dog 
10. The Parallax View 1974

Eraserhead 1977

Picnic at hanging rock 1975

Aguirre, the wrath of god 1972

Fitzcarraldo 1982

Walkabout 1971

Don't look now 1973

Blow up 1966 

Barton Fink 1991

The crying game 1992

Blue velvet 1986

Eyes Wide Shut 1999

Memories of murder 2003

Mother 2009

Chronicle 2012


Update: 

Wake in fright 1971

The clockwork orange 1971

Dear ringers 1988

Bitter moon 1992

American psycho 2000

The man who wasn't there 2001

A Scanner Darkly 2006

Disconnect 2012

The best offer 2013

Send help 2026

## 5. Please recommend movies where the quiet unassuming guy gets the girl.

**Score:** 20
**Body:** For example I love the movie Stranger Than Fiction. The guy has to be competent and just no one gives him a chance. I'd prefer this to be the A plotline but I might accept a really satisfying B plotline too :)

**EDIT: He should not be appealing!!! Like not traditionally... He should be an outcast a social pariah who everyone ignores. Ugly or overweight or disfigured or old = bonus points!**

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The 40-Year-Old Virgin
2. The Wedding Singer
3. The Perks of Being a Wallflower. The quiet kid story is done really well. It’s not over the top but it still hits.
4. She’s Out of My League (2010)
5. Hitch. Kevin James ends up with the girl, and he’s absolutely the quiet unassuming guy. Yeah it’s still the B plot, but only marginally.
6. About Time
7. Safety Not Guaranteed with Aubrey Plaza. Fits that almost to a T, but the romance might be considered a side plot.

Lars and the Real Girl has a very slight romance side plot with the MC,  who is quiet and shy

The Way, Way Back is a coming-of-age story with a shy, introverted teen who gets a girl. Has Steve Carell, Toni Collette, and Sam Rockwell.

Crazy, Stupid, Love- Steve Carell's character is unassuming and nice, but it only kind of fits because he goes through a lot of changes during the movie

While You Were Sleeping- Sandra Bullock and Bill Pullman. He's the quieter, less flashy brother who gets the girl, but it's not because no one gives him a chance. Cute movie though.
8. 13 Going on 30
9. While You Were Sleeping
10. Drive (2011).

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Daniel Brühl as Niki Lauda in Rush (2013) might be one of the greatest biopic performances ever put on screen.

**Score:** 2241

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Agreed. He's great and makes the film great imo.
  - Saw an interview with the real Nikki Lauda and I was stunned how accurate his portrayal was. It is criminal that he wasn’t nominated for an Oscar.
    - I think it becomes even more clear in the German version of the film. Brühl dubs his own voice in German, and he nails Niki's Austrian dialect like no Austrian ever thought a German could.
      - I am from Austria and that's absolutely true. When german trying to talk austrian dialect it mostly goes wrong because the way their A E I O and U sounds in words is heard miles away. So our specific austrian dialect words sound wrong. But D. Brühl is a professional, he had to practice a lot, i guess. Masterwork!
  - Yea, before watching rush, I thought he’ll be the rival with a villainous accent but he was the perfect athlete and stood his ground. Always wanted him to succeed throughout the film. 
2. This movie, and his performance (as well as Hemsworth’s), has always deserved more fanfare from the general public than it has gotten.

Real “Guess you’re not ready for that, but your kids are going to love it” Marty McFly vibes with it, tbh.

  - Just saw the trailer. Didn’t realize Ron Howard directed it. 

How did Rush go so under the radar in 2010?
3. ![gif](giphy|ZtCyOgzcYwBMCXvlAk)
4. An interesting example of how the film was sold on the idea of it being a thrilling race film with the classically handsome Hemsworth as the lead/focus, but in reality it was an ensemble between the two of them and quite obvious the screenwriter and director were more interested in and compelled by Lauda's life than James Hunt's.
  - Which is the right choice, great movie.

Surprisingly so. 

## 2. Is Sergeant Barnes from Platoon (1986) the darkest portrayal of an American soldier ever?

**Score:** 1287
**Body:** Barnes was a ruthless bastard in Platoon, and the scars on his face just added to the menace.

That dude was all about survival of the fittest at any cost.

If I had to give an armchair diagnosis, I’d say he was a psychopath with a sprinkle of narcissism. His willingness to kill Elias says everything about who he is.

Barnes saw Elias as a threat to his dominance, so eliminating him helped him maintain total control. Elias might’ve even been the whistleblower to expose Barnes’ misconduct.

But then again, he’s a complicated villain shaped by a brutal environment. Taylor caught him off guard in that vulnerable, reflective moment after the booby trap killed Sanderson.

He even showed a weird kind of protectiveness over his men. Even though Manny was in Elias’s camp, Barnes was enraged when he saw his mutilated body, and it lit the fuse in him to avenge Manny’s death.

Tom Berenger’s range is underrated. He can go from dramatic in Platoon to comedic in Major League. He was the perfect casting choice. Just a chilling portrayal.

“Y’all know about killing? I’d like to hear about it hotheads. Do y’all smoke this shit to escape from reality? Me, I don’t need this shit…I am reality."

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought Bunny was a pretty convincing psychopath.

https://preview.redd.it/ut64bz4vvwwg1.jpeg?width=600&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=3b63578459249c0925125218c0e746d9a73b78a5
  - "Did you see that head come apart, man!?"
    - Ain't nothing like a piece of pussy. Except maybe the indie500.
2. The door gunner in Full Metal Jacket. 


"How can you shoot women or children?"


"Easy! Ya just don't lead 'em so much! Ain't war hell?".
  - If they run they're VC.

If they don't run they're well trained VC.
    - Well disciplined 
3. I dunno... Sean Penn in Casualties of War is pretty fucking dark. 
4. ![gif](giphy|L7izd8RGHs5O0)

“Get some!” - Helicopter gunner, Full Metal Jacket

The machine gun operator is murdering civilians like it’s a video game

Completely detached from morality
5. Dad used to say that of Brando in Apocalypse Now.
  - Watch the outtakes of Brando from the making-of Apocalypse Now documentary, "Hearts of Darkness".     There's one where he's playing Kurtz and eating a piece of fruit and going on a monologue while interrogating Martin Sheen's character and he suddenly breaks character, coughs and looks right into the camera and says "I just ate a bug".   It's hilarious!

## 3. Jon Favreau admitting he was wrong about Iron Man’s death proves the Russos made the right call

**Score:** 1083
**Body:** Favreau was understandably protective since he started the MCU, but even he now admits that Tony’s sacrifice was handled perfectly. With the news of a 2026 *Endgame* re-release featuring *Avengers: Doomsday* bridge footage, it’s clear that this ending was necessary to set up the high stakes we’re seeing now.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There are no stakes in these movies. Infinity War was the last time there felt like any stakes at all it’s why it’s the best one

The introduction of a multiverse is horrible storytelling that udercuts the meaning of everything, It’s why the franchise has fallen off so hard 
  - I never took the time to stop and think about why I no longer care about Marvel movies. But reading this, I realize this is the reason. With the multi-verse, nothing matters. It seemed like freedom to the studio, I’m sure. But to fans, it just made the movies cheap and lazy. And I don’t mean the production value. 
    - >And I don’t mean the production value.

Although that has certainly dropped off too, especially the CGI.
  - I agree with this. The choice to go with the multiverse is a big reason I honestly lost pretty much all interest. 
2. There are zero stakes atm, because nothing’s been set up and most people stopped caring. 

All we know about Doom is stunt casting and a brief teaser in another universe at the end of F4. They’ll clearly try to force drama and stakes but it won’t come from buildup like Infinity War’s stakes did. 

  - This is the hundred million dollar answer right here for when Disney go trying to figure out why Doomsday underperformed. 
3. ![gif](giphy|bmAtIwmYTHnwBy0d6W)
4. What high stakes? With the multi versus we can have 1000x more iron man’s come back. There is 0% risk of anyone perma death 

Nice try John but your not selling Disney who just fired 1000 MCU employees get away it it 
  - You’re forgetting Uncle Ben. He’s the only one who stays dead.
5. High stakes, seriously? Marvel movies are about as lowest stakes as it comes. Enjoyable movies, mostly, where nothing really matters. Kind of like the scoring in whose line is it anyway.

**Score:** 932
**Body:** For me it’s the ending of Cast Away

1. The Stand By Me quote

"I never had friends later on like the ones I had when I was 12. Jesus, does anyone?"
2. ![gif](giphy|4GAUIfDrn0HFm)
3. I'll think about the end of the Mist from time to time 
  - this is literally what im thinking rn
4. Omg Cast Aways ending hits different every time 😭 mines the spinning top in Inception tho, still keeps me up at night lol
5. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind. 

“Meet me in Montauk” 

Chills down my spine 
6. https://preview.redd.it/ayihggrugxwg1.jpeg?width=780&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=4aa3b2826dbce190284ce7560650de382c96dc39

“I WAS CURED ALRIGHT…”

## 5. What’s a performance that completely deviates from the source material, but you still love it?

**Score:** 864
**Body:** What’s a movie performance that is completely different from the source material, whether fiction or historical, but you love it anyway? 

For me it’s Keanu Reeves in Constantine. In the comics he’s a wisecracking, devil may care blonde con artist from England. In the film a cynical, brooding antihero in LA. 

Completely different, but I still liked Keanus portrayal. 

What’s that one performance for you?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. STARSHIP TROOPERS

![gif](giphy|14g6PIAY8f6FeU)
2. John Hammond in Jurassic Park is a completely different, much more amiable, relatable, and less sociopathic character in the film than in the book, but I think it's all the better for that 
  - Is it true that the book is more of a horror story compared to the movie?
    - Not really, there's a lot more emphasis on characters, and the theme about the dinos reproducing in the wild is a more prominent plot point - but I'd actually argue the movie has all the hallmarks of a horror - the way the suspense builds, and you don't see the rex or raptors till later in the movie so the pay off is immense. Something totally lacking in all the other movies
    - The book can feel that way in the sense that dinosaurs are definitely not something you want to be around in real life and the way the dinosaurs kill people is not sanitized and you get to read the horror of a person's demise as they are eaten. It has spooky and thrilling moments but I wouldn't say it fully tips into horror. It has a horror-esque vibe though 
3. Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep is almost entirely different than Blade Runner.
4. Best. Satan. Ever.

I honestly think it's a huge loss we'll probably never see Peter Stormare again as Satan. 
  - “Lou… what took you so long??”
5. The Princess Bride


6. Liv Tyler as Arwen, vs. book Arwen.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Night of the Juggler (1980) non stop action in a very dirty New York City

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I happen to come upon this movie simply because the VHS artwork caught my attention. James Brolin’s gigantic, paranoid looking floating head. 

This movie exceeded the cover’s expectations. I’ve never heard of this one but oh my. If I could name a movie that the feel is similar to and you would know what I mean, it’s the Warriors. 

This film is set in the same gritty city but this New York city is so dirty it’s almost dystopian. No law and order but when there is, it’s insane. 

The story is simple. A crazy serial killer abducts a man’s daughter in front of him, man runs like a freight train for an hour and forty one minutes. Nothing, is going to stop him. Man James Brolin has the hair and beard of man that just lived in New York City during the 70’s. 

It’s almost non-stop action broken up with weird moments between the serial killer and daughter. He’s got gangs, cops and hot temper all following him as he is like a bull to save his daughter. 

The title and the cover sold me but the actual show made me type this recommendation. If you can find it, give it a go. It’s fun.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 2. The Departed as psychological drama and a film about identity

**Score:** 0
**Body:** The Departed is a compelling film for having a plot that makes space for two separate leading roles, each played by an A-list leading man. It is one plot, but the areas of activity of the two leads means that they don’t interact within the story for a very long time. Neither role is subordinate to the other. Both interact with the other huge star, the gang kingpin character. One of them spends more time with this figure, and can appear to be the film’s true lead, but the other is introduced earlier, and takes the film’s ending scene.

The rest of the film’s cast is also very impressive, and the different characters are all interesting foils to each of the two leads.

The Departed is a remake of bits of — as I understand — a whole trilogy of Hong Kong films, the Infernal Affairs trilogy. I haven’t seen these films. It’s also a Scorsese gangster film, and at 2.5 hours long, it has drawn comparison to Goodfellas and Casino, which are ‘based on true events’ sagas, which have some degree of a documentary or anthropological angle, in my opinion. I get a sense that where discussion of The Departed is concerned there tends to be a rush to comparison, and less time than one might expect, given the film’s popularity, devoted to simply discussing it as a film in itself. The Departed is different to Casino and Goodfellas because it’s a work of fiction, it has a plot, it essentially doesn’t have voice-over.

It has two male leads. I’d like to have a discussion of, basically, are they interesting characters? Do others find the film’s presentation of these two characters to be worth commenting on? Isn’t it quite a treat to have a film like this with an intricate plot, and roles for two young, in-their-prime men in which they respond to the plot’s circumstances — a field of play on which the two will express who they are? The cynical Colin and the existential Billy.

Do others find the characters convincing? Why are they drawn to be cops / gangsters? Is Matt Damon’s Colin a victim, groomed by the gangster Frank Costello, not given an alternative route to status before he has a chance to realize that he could work to attain what he wants in a legitimate way? Or is he a standard self-interested amoralist, living in a world, as it seems from the film, that is largely without values?

As for DiCaprio’s Billy, is it easy to get a sense from the film of what motivates him? Does he just want to test his mettle? If so, is that a symptom of a world largely without values? Or is there a compelling reading of his character, relating to identity, available? He shares the name of the film’s screenwriter, and also some traits. This is what William Monahan had to say about the film:

>**The thing about The Departed, the x-factor that people can’t quite put their finger on, is that it deals clearly with class and accent all these things that are fundamental to Boston, but previously anomalous or even prohibited in demotic American films. Without English art, I never would have understood myself, my own family, or the New England world I lived in, as it was at that time.**

>\[…\]

>**I’m more from a double world where I wasn’t part of anything or invested in anything, because I was Irish, and very Irish, but also the other part of my family, not that it had airs, or money, was descended from the first minister on Cape Ann in the 1620s. So in Boston terms I was everyone and no one, with no social investment, no social insecurity, sort of Imitation of Christ in one hand and The Education of Henry Adams in the other, and because I was part of nothing I could observe everything without having anything personal invested in the findings. I was born in 1960 and there were still sadistic old ladies, pretend Yankees with their fucking hats pinned on, born in the previous century, who would treat you like a cute little Papist, even after Kennedy was president. And I was a mick but I was also more them than they were.**

There is definitely plenty of gestures to ideas of identity or ethnic stereotype in the film. There is essentially a laying out of Billy‘s biography in the film, some of which overlaps what Monahan describes here. Is The Departed’s Billy an intriguing character study? Is there anything interesting in the references to ethnicity in the film? Is Billy a standard James Dean outsider type, or is he more mature and controlled than that?

Is The Departed a genuine psychological drama, given its portraits of its two leads, or its four main characters, the gangster and the female psychologist love interest included?

Anyway, I wanted to put these questions out there because I don’t know whether these things are much discussed regarding The Departed.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That bit from Monahan sums it up pretty nicely regarding Billy, who is helpfully laid completely bare by Dignam in the first act (Dignam sees everyone for what they are, and what they're nawt). It's a psychological portrait of two people aspiring to belong to other worlds and dying for it. One "shanty Irish" cop who will do anything for a high rise Beacon Hill apartment. One "lace curtain Irish" who tried to find himself in the footsteps of his blue collar father instead of following his own potential.

Both frauds. The "lace curtain Irish" kid is shot by a uniformed cop, teaching him that his father's morality didn't live in his blue collar. He's tossed in a casket and given his working class cop funeral: flag draped, bagpipes playing, and it feels like a funeral for someone who never even existed. 

The "shanty Irish" kid dies alone, shot by the vengeful ghost of the old neighborhood, right in front of his nice curtains.

I think it's a great study of the identity loss that happens with class mobility in any direction. Sons looking for the approval of their father's generation in all the wrong places. 
  - Great comment, and a very interesting contrast to the other comment so far, which I’ll quote.

>In terms of character development and motivation… I must have missed it if there is any?

>There’s some great cinematic tension driven by the plot, but I couldn’t really point to anything much within the characters (beyond them generally being pretty volatile) that adds to it.

This contrast illustrates something I was trying to get at with my post. There are themes relating to ethnic identity and class mobility, as you point out, that are in the film, and in a way that is very explicit. But is this material somehow offered up in a way that’s it’s just very easy to ignore?

I also think that it’s very easy to get involved in the situations of the two main character — and that is to say to become interested by their mere personal or psychological identities, and how those will develop or drive them as the action develops — in a way that is completely to one side of questions of what type of Irish-Americans they are. The Departed is really beautifully plotted, in my opinion, but I feel like that could be emphasized a bit more in discussion of it. Maybe it’s not because the plot or premise is largely borrowed.

I’m also interested by the exact meaning of ‘lace-curtain Irish’. Does it mean highly respectable morally — and snobbish about that perhaps? Or is there a component of wealth meant by the label too?

    - The modest wealth, and the attendant implications of "putting on airs", is the only thing implied. The term, and its opposite "shanty Irish" which isn't actually spoken in the film, are both pejorative, so I definitely wouldn't go asking someone if their branch of the Sullivans is one or the other. 
2. I find it a really fun film with a great premise, an amazing cast and some great quirks in the production and soundtrack, but the acting performances and the dialogue really let it down.

I rewatched it recently with quite high expectations but it’s kind of hard to take seriously? Matt Damon is good, Jack Nicholson is Jack Nicholson, but Leo and so many of the supporting cast I just don’t buy at all.

In terms of character development and motivation… I must have missed it if there is any? There must be some, but I’m struggling to recall why anyone is really doing what they’re doing.

There’s some great cinematic tension driven by the plot, but I couldn’t really point to anything much within the characters (beyond them generally being pretty volatile) that adds to it.
  - I appreciate this take. Your take represents one not entirely uncommon view of the film.

But, combined with the fast editing, I think the dialogue comes across as for one thing, representing a fully fleshed out sensibility of the screenwriter, and two, it’s funny, I think. That is, I would definitely say that dialogue is a highlight of the film, not a low-light, but of course it can strike others differently.

  - I find it a lazy work for Scorsese. Not one single idea that hasn’t been used and reused in his career (even the score is recycled). Infernal Affairs, at least, is direct to the point and don’t tire you for 2.5 hours.

## 3. Is there a name for the type of film Sam Raimi made, where there's one protagonist and one antagonist?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Hey guys,

I am not that familiar with film as a medium. However, I enjoyed the Sam Raimi films. In the Spider-man film its clear that who the protagonist is and who is the antagonist. I have watched other films both superheroes and others and its not quite clear. There's many characters and all of them have different story lines. 

Also is there an influence of theater on this kind of films were the focus is more on one or two characters as opposed to multiple one's?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I feel like that's just most films. Movies where there are many protagonists are ensemble films, specifically as an exception to the default where there is a single main character. Superhero movies tend to be *especially* clear on this one, outside of team-up films. Sometimes they have multiple villains, or the hero has a sidekick, but is pretty clear cut who the main protagonist and antagonist are. 
  - a common approach for villains is to have the main villain and the heavy. where the heavy is the scary physical guy, but the true villain is the brains behind the operation. very common in James Bond films, scharzenneger films, etc.
2. Based on the title, I really thought you were talking about *Send Help* here.

Back before the MCU era it was common nerd wisdom that “multiple villains” was a sign that your superhero franchise was getting stale and desperate. Batman fighting the Joker? Good. Batman fighting Mr. Freeze, Poison Ivy, and Bane? Bad. Spider-Man fighting the Green Goblin? Good. Spider-Man fighting Sandman, Venom, and a different Green Goblin? Bad. The X-Men fighting Magneto? Good. The X-Men fighting Magneto, the US government, and corrupted Jean Grey? Bad.

I think the reason it made sense at the time is that that generation of superhero movies tended to be more driven by big setpieces and particular showcased special effects, so you wanted a simple, iconic story around it. (I think this is less of a “theater influence” and more of the influence of an older, pulpier generation of superhero comics.) Crowding the field usually meant you were bringing in a bunch of characters to sell Happy Meal toys but the actual movie would rush through all that stuff without delivering on any of it.

In the MCU era, the action and effects usually aren’t a big draw on their own, and people watch superhero movies as a big twisty soap opera, so more characters is generally considered a positive. I’d suggest watching more old superhero movies if you like the Raimi approach best: The Rocketeer, 70s Superman, Burton Batman.
  - \> The X-Men fighting Magneto, the US government, and corrupted Jean Grey? 

I really liked Last Stand, but maybe I'm the only one. I enjoy the theme that the true villain is the government who wants to eliminate all mutants, and magneto/professor X have two different approaches to solving this problem. Like MLK and Malcolm X.
3. I feel like this is most films, but what you're fixated on is the blatant juxtaposition between a primary protagonist and antagonist. Yin & yang. Batman and Joker are probably the poster children for this.

Maybe Gladiator or Heat?

Return of the Jedi?

## 4. CMV: All writer-directors decline in their fifties

**Score:** 0
**Body:** When we speak of longevity of filmmakers, we keep pointing to Steven Spielberg, Martin Scorsese as examples. But they aren't writers, mostly. I cannot really think of any solo writer-director who has put out masterpieces past their forties. Not about box office hits or awards, but films that will endure and be discussed with their best work. The Wolf of Wall Street for Scorsese is an example.

James Cameron from Avatar

Coen Brothers from Burn After Reading

Wong Kar-Wai from My Blueberry Nights

Quentin Tarantino from The Hateful Eight. OUATIH was Oscar bait and an indulgent mess.

Bong Joon-ho from Mickey17

Wes Anderson from The French Dispatch

Paul Thomas Anderson from Licorice Pizza. OBAA was Oscar bait and nowhere near PTA in his element.

Christopher Nolan from Tenet. Oppenheimer was Oscar bait. And The Odyssey will be a mess too.

The only possible counter-example I can think of is Mad Max Fury Road, written by guys in their sixties/seventies and directed by 70-year-old George Miller

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lynch and Godard both remained stubbornly themselves into ripened age. Some of my favorite works from either are well beyond their 50s. 


I feel that your criteria for "losing the plot" is pretty narrow and presumptive, though. I mean, people called Magnolia oscar-bait and that was PTA's third film. I'm not sure I agree that the best dichotomy to judge a director's consistency is perceived pandering.
2. Not a real criticism. Calling good movies “Oscar bait” doesn’t make them bad. There’s always going to be a subset of films that are made to garner Oscar type buzz, but that doesn’t mean they are fundamentally bad films. These films aren’t even close to my favorite films as at this stage in my life either as I usually prefer lower budget and indie films, but that doesn’t make those films inherently bad. Oppenheimer is a good film. I think the argument has merit to say they decline, but the way you go about it isn’t valid criticism. 

There’s also people like Sean baker. The coens wrote bridge of spies. And it all comes down to opinions, I found OUATIH to be better than movies like the hateful eight because the overdone violence by Tarantino got old.

  - Plus, using the term Oscar bait emphasizes that you've really narrowed your scope down to successful mainstream but auteur type writer directors who hit the ground running and built a recognizable brand.

What you're describing are franchise players, and you're deriding them for doing what just about everybody wants them to do, which is make movies for their fans.

7. This post makes me kinda sad. I'm gonna write a bit to (hopefully) make myself feel better, and maybe change your mind. You can tell me if it works!

First, I disagree with your premise and your specific examples.

An easy place to start is to take the Coen brothers: While it's true that 'No County for Old Men' is often considered their best and 'Burn After Reading' freakin' rips, they followed that film with 'A Serious Man', 'True Grit' and 'Inside Llewyn Davis'. All of which, in my opinion, are excellent films. For that matter, the other contender for best Coen brothers "best" is 'A Serious Man'. To add to that, while I think Ethan Coen's more recent work has been pleasant but underwhelming, I found Joel Coen's 'The Tragedy of MacBeth' dazzling.

Now let's look at your others specific 'weak' films. They include a massive formal experiment by way of a 3-hour Russian montage film about perhaps the second most consequential physicist in history (Oppenheimer), an impressionistic political satire-cum-high octane thriller-cum-multigenerational character study (One Battle After Another), and a sprawling, meandering hangout film blended with historical fiction (Once Upon a Time in Hollywood). All of these were artistic risks. Why do you think of them as 'Oscar bait'? Because they succeeded? Because they garnered Oscar attention? I would encourage you to look past this mindset. These are serious filmmakers dedicated to their craft and, while winning an Oscar is certainly useful to any filmmaker, I promise that isn't their reason for making these films. Try to watch them again, but, this time, imagine that the Oscars don't even exist, and *then* ask yourself why they might have made these films. I think you'll get a lot out of the experience.

Finally, let's get to the meat of your argument - that writer-directors weaken as they grow old. Well, they certainly do. After all, it's taxing and exhausting to conceptualize and create a film. And one day each one of us will grow too old to meet the task, no matter how good at it we may be today.

But why create this arbitrary cutoff date? The truth is people age at different rates. And for many years it's not your age that determines your ability, but how much work you're willing to put in to maintain it. You note Wes Anderson, but I think his short film series for netflix is some of the best and most experimental work he's ever done (the caveat there being, of course, that he was adapting another's stories). A few more examples of post-50's writer directors, to whet your whistle:

1. Pedro Almodóvar, born 1949, wrote and directed 'Talk to Her' and 'The Skin I Live In' at 52 and 61 respectively, two of his most popular and acclaimed works
2. Agnes Varda, born 1928, directed the documentaries 'The Gleaners and I' and 'Faces Places' at 72 and 87! Which, given that she also edited them, indicates that her age posed no threat to her storytelling prowess.
3. Orson Welles wrote, directed and edited the fascinating 'F for Fake' at 59 which, given that it's a surreal, innovated quasi-documentary/cinematic magic trick, does not indicate that he was slowing down
4. To separate the art from artist for a moment: Woody Allen, born 1935, wrote and directed the absolute bangers 'The Purple Rose of Cairo', 'Hannah and Her Sisters', 'Radio Days' and 'September' successively from 50-53. Plus he continued to innovate for many years with films like 'Husbands and Wives', 'Match Point' and 'Midnight in Paris' much later. Though I will admit that, with Allen, there was a pretty steep drop off in quality films after his tear through the 70's and 80's.
5. Stanley Kubrick of course, who co-wrote and directed 'The Shining' at 50, 'Full Metal Jacket' at 57, and 'Eyes Wide Shut' at freaking 70! (And please, no 'Eyes Wide Shut' slander here. If you didn't like it, go rewatch it, it gets better each time.)
6. Let's not forget David Lynch, who wrote and directed 'Mulholland Drive' at 55, and blessed us with an excellent revival a classic 25 years later - no small task - "Twin Peaks: the Return", co-writing and directing an entire television season at the ripe age of 71!
7. And, of course, the grand-daddy of them all, without who's late-life work the entire field of narrative cinema would not be the same: Akira Kurosawa. He wrote, co-write and directed 'The Bad Sleep Well', 'Yojimbo', 'High and Low', 'Ran' and 'Dreams' at 50, 51, 53, 65 and 70, all of which can go toe-to-toe with any of his early-period masterpieces.

And more! Milos Forman co-writes and directs 'Amadeus' at 52; Andrea Arnold writes and directs 'American Honey' at 55, and more recently the excellent (in my opinion) 'Bird' at 63; Jacques Tati writes, directs and stars in his magnum opus 'Playtime' at 60.

What I hope you'll see here is that, while a factor, age is no barrier. Indeed, it seems like when it comes to our favorite directors, dropping off after 50 could be the *exception.* And what a wonderful thing! It indicates what's obvious already - that good films come from good ideas, and a good idea is liable to drop into anyone's head, no matter their age. And, so long as you've been working hard, keeping your energy up, there's no reason to think you couldn't be the one to bring that idea to life.
8. From Mickey 17? How many movies did he make since?

What is Oscar bait anyway? And what about OBAA, Oppenheimer or OUATIH fit the criteria? Calling a movie award bait is lazy criticism. If there are specific problems then say what those are. Award bait is too vague.

I’m an unapologetic fan of the Avatar movies but I understand the criticism for its writing. That said, in all the movies except the first one Cameron is a co-writer. 

PTA, Coens, Bong Joon-ho, Tarantino etc are not journeyman filmmakers who make standard films. They’re great because they keep pushing themselves and that’s the most important thing I want from them. I’ll take five misfires if I get a great film in return.
9. I don't think your post is as ridiculous as many commenters are making out, and I agree with you about the directors you've listed. Having said that, two of my GOAT directors made their most celebrated films in their 50s and 60s - Kiarostami made Close Up when he was 50 and Taste Of Cherry at 57. Maurice Pialat made The Mouth Agape at 50 and went to make To Our Loves, Under The Sun Of Satan, and Lou Lou. Agnes Varda made Vagabond at 57 and The Gleaners And I at 72! Ingmar Bergman made many iconic films in his 50s and far beyond. So yeh, many important exceptions to the rule there.
